item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one really caters to my love for computers. I just have to upvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, why would it run Linux? That's so weird

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, I understand using █ to block out Sites, Agents, years, etc…

But why the hell did this need to █ the number of choral songs??? Is that dangerous? Is it information that isn't needed? What is the harm in saying that there were 1,642 songs on the device? And that 335 were not in existance?

It's not that interesting to me. I've read so many scips that were just *creepy computers* and this one didn't do it for me.

Downvoted -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This just seems meh, i like the idea of it tapping into other realities every time it boots up but it feels a little lackluster. The virus is neat and the bank details are a nice clue but the other addendum just seem like dead weight, adding something in like crazy USA propaganda fallout style perhaps ant-deviate war recruitment or even something suggesting a foundation based world takeover in another reality would make it better. A good article that can still be improved

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like to see the original, if anyone has it lying around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This should be safe not euclid. Don't switch it on, no problems.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the fact it's running Linux in Latin. That's weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In its current form, this appears to pass locked-box - so shouldn't it be Safe rather than Euclid?

Other than that - IMHO this is a poorly-executed pun on the "quantum computer" - it's not clever and not even really interesting to me.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I should have been expecting it, but I wasn't - too non-techie, I guess. But it would have been awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was definitely looking/waiting for it to pop up myself.

Change Apple OS X to [REDACTED] and everyone could assume it's Faithful OS. =P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, you definitely weren't the only one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wasn't liking where it was going at first, but the overall result got an upvote out of me anyway. Just something about it.

Also, was I the only one who was expecting "Faithful OS" (SCP-093) to show up as one of the test results?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On re-read, I've upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thought it was somewhat interesting. It came across to me as a computer that syncs up with parallel realities every time it boots up. Some things are close but still off like the bank statements while you also have an instance where it linked with some completely unknown virus that can spontaneously jump machines. I think the Euclid status is good considering it has the potential to access things outside of our reality and there is no way of knowing how dangerous it could be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to agree with Agent A. Smith, I can't see how this wouldn't be classified as "Safe", but then, I'm new.
Also, while neat, and certainly an anomaly, I can't find any part of this that makes it truly an SCP over just another weird object with weird properties that doesn't have much value or danger attached. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm wondering what virus it has that does this as the entire article does not mention anything at all about it being infected with something. Only thing remotely close as to leading up to this virus announcement was to never connect it to an external network. I believe this SCP can be improved further though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it better than the old one, but I don't know why it is Euclid. Doesn't it pass the locked box test? The last entry implies that it has some security risk, but not enough to be creepy. If we knew, for example, that it infected the other computers while isolated by a Faraday cage and 100 meters of rock, it would be more threatening.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I … sorta … agree with this. On the other hand, I like the current execution enough that I'm not downvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty much this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In my opinion, all you did was swap out old clichés for new clichés. It's just another "OMG! How does it know this? WTF?". I don't know if I can put this in concrete terms, but the new one has just as little mystery as the old one, except the reason why is different: in the original, there was one possibility (computer from the future). In the new one, there are no possibilities, so the only option left is magic. Not as in, wizard magic, as in, narrative magic. It does what it does because shut up.

I'm sorry, but this is (in my opinion) no better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's also good to see one in 1-100 improve, since those are probably the first ones new folks read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm always glad to see an old one get saved. It was really forgettable before, now it is neat.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Agreed, I've just upvoted it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And when Dr. King uses it…

It's better then it was, that's certain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the rewrite and changed my vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: MUCH better. Upvoted.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Voting against. The rewrite isn't bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The rewrite has been posted. Since its old form was too similar to 155 and bordered on far too many cliches, I decided to instead go for a play on the idea of a "quantum" computer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seeing as this one is a "classic", I'm going to take a shot at reworking this. Draft will be up soon.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
I guess it's time to sweep this one under the rug then, voting for deletion.

I can't even remember if this relates to anything else to qualify for ARCin'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Keep the downvotes coming, this seriously needs revamping.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The SCP currently sits at -4. It isn't scary. Any intrigue the concept may have had is squandered when instead of dropping hints, slowly building tension, the author says "Yeah, it's probably from the future".

If this article has any potential, it's as a -J, where the joke is that instead of it being a super-advanced future computer, it's a shitty laptop from the nineties that the Foundation found in the fifties, but that might be borrowing too heavily from 711-EX.

Excuse me while I write that, and downvote this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh, if it inspires enough people, I'm sure it'll be -ARCed.

But if it doesn't…

So if you've got ideas to save it, just go for it.

I'm not sure how, and really it's on the author (or not our place?) to fix it.

It's our place if the author gives permission, or if the author vanishes and an administrator (maybe moderators too? I don't actually know that) gives permission.

And this administrative permission has already been given, as you can see from Quik's comment on page 1.

Or we can let it die, of course.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree- this /could/ be good (less bad?). I'm not sure how, and really it's on the author (or not our place?) to fix it.

Don't delete it until someone has a chance to try and fix it, then delete it when they fucked it up? lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one was an import, right? Author unknown or lost in time? I ask because all that I'm seeing is (effectively) a locked that's got "SEKRETS" written on the outside that cannot be opened.

I probably upvoted it as it was one of the first few I came across here, and there's some potential here (Why is it here? What other secrets does it hold? Is it related to any of the time traveling SCPs contained, is it cross dimensional, is it from an unknown past, or something weirder?)

Personally, I'd like to see someone take this and do something with it. I think it really needs saving

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree that it's better, but it's still uninteresting and not scary in any way; ergo, my downvote remains. I will watch for adoption, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As the humorous addenda have been removed, so has my downvote been removed. Will watch to see if someone adopts this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Before this sinks too much further, I've lopped the three humorous addenda, leaving just the actual SCP. It's… um, rather shorter. (There's a writing lesson in there— take the humorous addenda out of your article, and what, really, do you have left?)

I think this is salvageable, but it's gonna take something (or several somethings) to set it apart. Anyone wanna give it a whack before its drops to Deletionville?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fortunately for me, I hadn't. This is in the negatives at time of writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why did I upvoted this? I don't find the reason, I read and read… but… Yeah Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Then allow me. How the hell has this lasted so long with THREE lolfoundation quips?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I tried to downvote this, and realized I already had. Damn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've seen better objects being downvoted and deleted in no time
Yet this one remains here for some reason.
I tried to find some redeeming features in this object - and found none. Even the humorous addenda are old and worn out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just not very interesting, and then there's three - count 'em, three - humorous addenda. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kind of like time-traveling SCPs (and have written a couple myself) but this one is not good. Minmin is 100% right about the tone and the improper use of D-Class…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, that's our job. You need special training to be as good at being a dick as Yoric and myself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is a shitty SCP, but don't be a dick.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't really think this fits in well with the on-site articles in that it's basically not interesting nor creepy, and in addition to that it uses cliche number 16 (thing from the future).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm guessing that there's an unusually high probability that future artifacts will be Foundation related, because the Foundation is one of the few groups with both the technology and the cojones to mess around with time travel. The description makes it sound to me like the room was uncovered during construction work on a Foundation site, and was probably from a future version of that same site. In that case the computer was probably not originally anything special; it's standard future-tech office equipment that qualifies as an SCP by our standards. So it would naturally have SCP-related files on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This should be a -j. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How exactly would you attempt to install it in the first place? The thing doesn't even have a disc drive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Crysis had high system requirements at time of release (at least if you wanted the highest graphic details.) That's pretty much it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can somebody explain the Crysis joke to me? I mean, is it anything to do with the game itself?

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I suppose… I guess it just seems a bit overly-fortuitous that they were able to find such a thing.

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded:
Actually, quite a few of the future items contain data on SCPs.

I believe the general consensus of the writers is that most of the SCP-classified future debris are classified *as* SCPs *because* they contain data on SCPs. I mean, just a simple future iPod would probably just be tossed into a box in a warehouse with all the others and stamped 'Future Materials, Do Not Open Until [DATA EXPUNGED]', or simply sent to a Foundation lab to be reverse-engineered. Or maybe just simply tossed into a furnace.

At least, that's my take on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I might be a complete and utter retard on this, so feel free to call me on it, but…

…a sleek, futuristic computer falls into the hands of SCP and, upon (finally) figuring out how it works, we find out that it contains encrypted documents about… SCPs?

Doesn't that seem a little too obvious?

item.title: Open source:
item.content:encoded: Even euclid-class objects know it's good.
